vector_spaces.label: Vector spaces: the rules that make linear algebra work
vector_spaces.summary: Learn the axioms behind vector spaces and why closure, the zero vector, and scalar multiplication matter.

sketch.title: What makes a set a vector space?

subspaces.summary: Understand when a subset of a vector space inherits the same structure and becomes a subspace.

inverse.summary: Learn what an inverse matrix does, when it exists, and how it undoes a transformation.

sketch.title: Special directions that keep their line

vector_spaces.title: Vector spaces: the rules behind the whole subject
vector_spaces.bodyMarkdown:
A **vector space** is not just a pile of vectors. It is a set together with rules for **addition** and **scalar multiplication** that behave in a very controlled way.

Why do mathematicians care so much about these rules? Because once a set satisfies them, all the powerful tools of linear algebra become available.

---

## The big idea

A vector space must let you:
- add two vectors and stay inside the set
- multiply a vector by a scalar and stay inside the set
- use a zero vector
- use negatives and algebra rules consistently

That means the space is stable under the operations you want to perform.

---

## Why this matters

If a collection is not a vector space, then many linear algebra methods break.
You lose closure, you lose structure, and formulas stop making sense.

That is why vector spaces are the first major abstraction of the subject:
not because they are fancy, but because they tell you **when the machinery is allowed to run**.

---

## Common examples

- $\mathbb{R}^n$
- polynomials of degree at most $n$
- continuous functions
- matrices of a fixed size

The same theory works in all of them because they satisfy the same structural rules.

subspaces.title: Subspaces: smaller vector spaces inside bigger ones
subspaces.bodyMarkdown:
A **subspace** is a subset of a vector space that is itself still a vector space under the same operations.

That means you do **not** invent new addition or scalar multiplication. You inherit them from the larger space.

---

## The quick test

To check whether a subset is a subspace, you usually verify three things:

1. it contains the zero vector
2. it is closed under addition
3. it is closed under scalar multiplication

If any one of these fails, it is not a subspace.

---

## Geometric intuition

Inside $\mathbb{R}^2$, a line through the origin is a subspace.
A line that does **not** pass through the origin is not.

Inside $\mathbb{R}^3$:
- the origin alone is a subspace
- a line through the origin is a subspace
- a plane through the origin is a subspace
- the whole space is a subspace

---

## Why subspaces matter

Subspaces are where solutions live.
Null spaces, column spaces, row spaces, eigenspaces — all of these are subspaces.

So once you understand subspaces, you begin to understand where linear algebra stores its answers.

linear_combinations.title: Linear combinations: building new vectors from old ones
linear_combinations.bodyMarkdown:
A **linear combination** is what you get when you scale vectors and then add them.

$$
a_1v_1 + a_2v_2 + \cdots + a_kv_k
$$

This is one of the deepest ideas in linear algebra because it tells you how vectors are **constructed**.

---

## Span

The **span** of a set of vectors is the collection of **all** linear combinations of those vectors.

So span answers a reachability question:

> Starting from these vectors, what can I build?

---

## Geometry view

In $\mathbb{R}^2$:
- one nonzero vector spans a line through the origin
- two nonparallel vectors span the whole plane

In $\mathbb{R}^3$:
- one vector spans a line
- two independent vectors span a plane
- three independent vectors span the whole space

---

## Why span matters

Span tells you whether your vectors are enough to describe a space.
It is one half of the basis idea.
The other half is independence.

linear_independence.title: Linear independence: when no vector is redundant
linear_independence.bodyMarkdown:
A collection of vectors is **linearly independent** when none of them can be built from the others.

Equivalently, the equation

$$
a_1v_1 + a_2v_2 + \cdots + a_kv_k = 0
$$

has only the trivial solution

$$
a_1 = a_2 = \cdots = a_k = 0.
$$

---

## Intuition

Independence means every vector contributes something new.
Dependence means at least one vector is redundant.

So independence is really about **information content**.

---

## Geometry view

In $\mathbb{R}^2$:
- two vectors are independent if they are not multiples of each other

In $\mathbb{R}^3$:
- three vectors are independent if none lies in the plane spanned by the other two

---

## Why it matters

If your vectors are dependent, you are carrying extra baggage.
If they are independent, you have a clean, efficient set of building blocks.

basis_dimension.title: Basis and dimension: the smallest complete description
basis_dimension.bodyMarkdown:
A **basis** is a set of vectors that is both:
- linearly independent
- spanning

That means the basis vectors are enough to build the whole space, but none of them is redundant.

---

## Why basis is so important

A basis is the ideal coordinate system.
It gives you the smallest clean set of building blocks for the whole space.

Every vector in the space can be written uniquely as a combination of basis vectors.

---

## Dimension

The **dimension** of a vector space is the number of vectors in any basis.

That number measures how many independent directions the space has.

Examples:
- $\mathbb{R}^2$ has dimension 2
- $\mathbb{R}^3$ has dimension 3
- the space of polynomials of degree at most 2 has dimension 3

---

## Big picture

Span tells you whether your vectors are enough.
Independence tells you whether they are redundant.
Basis is where those two ideas meet.

transpose.title: Transpose: rows become columns
transpose.bodyMarkdown:
The **transpose** of a matrix flips its rows and columns.

If $A$ is an $m \times n$ matrix, then $A^T$ is an $n \times m$ matrix.

---

## Intuition

Transpose is like viewing the same data from the other direction.
What used to be a row becomes a column, and what used to be a column becomes a row.

---

## Why transpose matters

Transpose appears everywhere:
- dot products
- symmetric matrices
- least squares
- orthogonality
- matrix identities

---

## Important rules

$$
(A+B)^T = A^T + B^T
$$

$$
(AB)^T = B^T A^T
$$

That second rule is especially important: when transposing a product, the order reverses.

determinant.title: Determinant: scaling, orientation, and invertibility
determinant.bodyMarkdown:
The **determinant** is a single number attached to a square matrix.
It tells you how the matrix scales area or volume and whether the transformation collapses space.

---

## Geometry meaning

For a $2 \times 2$ matrix, the determinant tells you the signed area scaling.
For a $3 \times 3$ matrix, it tells you signed volume scaling.

If the determinant is zero, the transformation squashes space into a lower dimension.
That means information is lost.

---

## Orientation

A positive determinant keeps orientation.
A negative determinant flips orientation.

---

## Why determinant matters

A square matrix is invertible exactly when its determinant is nonzero.
So determinant is one of the fastest ways to test whether a matrix can be undone.

inverse.title: Inverse: undoing what a matrix does
inverse.bodyMarkdown:
If a matrix $A$ has an inverse, then there exists a matrix $A^{-1}$ such that

$$
A^{-1}A = AA^{-1} = I.
$$

That means applying $A$ and then $A^{-1}$ brings you back to where you started.

---

## Intuition

Think of $A$ as a transformation.
The inverse is the transformation that perfectly undoes it.

But not every matrix has an inverse.
If a transformation crushes two different inputs to the same output, there is no way to uniquely reverse it.

---

## When does the inverse exist?

For square matrices:
- inverse exists iff determinant is nonzero

---

## Why it matters

Inverses help solve systems, reverse transformations, and understand when information is preserved.

eigenvalues_eigenvectors.title: Eigenvalues and eigenvectors: the special directions of a matrix
eigenvalues_eigenvectors.bodyMarkdown:
Most vectors change direction when a matrix acts on them.
But some special vectors keep their line.
They may stretch, shrink, or flip — but they do not turn away from that line.

Those are **eigenvectors**.
The associated scale factors are **eigenvalues**.

---

## Definition

A nonzero vector $v$ is an eigenvector of $A$ if

$$
Av = \lambda v
$$

for some scalar $\lambda$.

---

## Why this matters

Eigenvectors reveal the natural directions of a transformation.
Eigenvalues reveal how strongly the matrix acts in those directions.

These ideas power:
- differential equations
- data science and PCA
- quantum mechanics
- network ranking
- stability analysis

---

## Key intuition

Most vectors get mixed.
Eigenvectors are the ones that do not get mixed — only scaled.
That is why they are so important.

la_m2_vector_space_axioms.title: Vector space axioms
la_m2_vector_space_axioms.prompt: Which description best captures what a vector space must allow?
la_m2_vector_space_axioms.hint: Think about closure, the zero vector, and scalar multiplication.

options.c: Addition and scalar multiplication that satisfy the vector space rules

la_m2_vector_space_zero_vector.title: Zero vector requirement
la_m2_vector_space_zero_vector.prompt: Why must a vector space contain a zero vector?
la_m2_vector_space_zero_vector.hint: The zero vector is part of the additive structure.

options.a: Because every vector space must have an additive identity
options.b: Because every vector must have norm 0
options.c: Because scalar multiplication is impossible otherwise
options.d: Because bases always contain only the zero vector

la_m2_vector_space_not_every_set.title: Not every set is a vector space
la_m2_vector_space_not_every_set.prompt: Why is not every collection of objects automatically a vector space?
la_m2_vector_space_not_every_set.hint: The operations must stay inside the set and obey the rules.

options.a: Because vectors must always be arrows in 2D
options.b: Because the set may fail closure or other axioms
options.c: Because only matrices can form vector spaces
options.d: Because scalar multiplication is never allowed

la_m2_vector_space_rn_example.title: A standard example
la_m2_vector_space_rn_example.prompt: Which of the following is a standard vector space?
la_m2_vector_space_rn_example.hint: Think of the most familiar example from linear algebra.

options.a: Only the positive vectors in R^n
options.b: Only vectors of length 1 in R^n

la_m2_vector_space_function_example.title: Functions as vectors
la_m2_vector_space_function_example.prompt: Why can many sets of functions be treated as vector spaces?
la_m2_vector_space_function_example.hint: Because you can add functions and scale them while staying in the set.

options.b: Because addition and scalar multiplication can satisfy the axioms
options.d: Because all functions are automatically eigenvectors

la_m2_subspace_definition.title: Subspace definition
la_m2_subspace_definition.hint: It is a subset that is still a vector space under the same operations.

options.a: Any subset with at least two vectors
options.c: A subset that is itself a vector space under the inherited operations
options.d: Any subset closed under subtraction only

la_m2_subspace_zero_required.title: Why zero matters
la_m2_subspace_zero_required.prompt: Why must a subspace contain the zero vector?
la_m2_subspace_zero_required.hint: A subspace is still a vector space.

options.a: Because every vector space must contain its additive identity
options.b: Because zero is the only eigenvector
options.c: Because dimension would be undefined otherwise
options.d: Because it must contain every vector

la_m2_subspace_line_through_origin.title: Line through the origin
la_m2_subspace_line_through_origin.prompt: Which line in R^2 is a subspace?
la_m2_subspace_line_through_origin.hint: The origin is the deciding detail.

options.c: A line parallel to the x-axis but not through the origin
options.d: No line can be a subspace

la_m2_subspace_span_is_subspace.title: Span and subspaces
la_m2_subspace_span_is_subspace.prompt: What is always true about the span of a set of vectors?

options.c: It always equals the whole ambient space

la_m2_subspace_polynomial_example.title: Polynomial example
la_m2_subspace_polynomial_example.prompt: Which set is a subspace of the space of all polynomials?
la_m2_subspace_polynomial_example.hint: Look for closure under addition and scalar multiplication.

la_m2_linear_combination_definition.title: Linear combination
la_m2_linear_combination_definition.prompt: What is a linear combination of vectors?

options.a: A sum of scalar multiples of the vectors
options.b: A determinant built from the vectors

options.c: All vectors obtainable as linear combinations

la_m2_linear_combination_compute_input.title: Compute a linear combination
la_m2_linear_combination_compute_input.hint: Multiply first, then add component by component.

la_m2_span_membership.title: Membership in a span
la_m2_span_membership.prompt: To say a vector lies in the span of a set means:

options.a: It is orthogonal to every vector in the set
options.b: It can be written as a linear combination of the set
options.c: It must be one of the original vectors exactly
options.d: It has the same norm as the original vectors

la_m2_coefficients_role.title: Role of coefficients
la_m2_coefficients_role.prompt: What do the coefficients in a linear combination do?
la_m2_coefficients_role.hint: They control how much of each vector you use.

options.d: They scale the vectors before addition

la_m2_linear_independence_definition.title: Linear independence
la_m2_linear_independence_definition.prompt: What does it mean for vectors to be linearly independent?

options.a: They all have the same length
options.c: Only the trivial combination gives the zero vector
options.d: They span the whole ambient space

la_m2_dependence_nontrivial_relation.title: Dependence and nontrivial relations
la_m2_dependence_nontrivial_relation.prompt: A set of vectors is linearly dependent exactly when:

options.a: A nonzero coefficient combination produces the zero vector

la_m2_independence_geometric_r2.title: Geometric test in R^2
la_m2_independence_geometric_r2.prompt: When are two vectors in R^2 linearly independent?

options.b: When neither is a scalar multiple of the other

la_m2_independence_matrix_example.title: Dependent or independent?
la_m2_independence_matrix_example.hint: If one vector is a scalar multiple of the other, the set is dependent.

la_m2_dependence_zero_vector.title: The zero vector and dependence
la_m2_dependence_zero_vector.prompt: What happens if a set contains the zero vector along with other vectors?
la_m2_dependence_zero_vector.hint: The zero vector creates an immediate nontrivial relation.

options.d: The set is automatically linearly dependent

options.b: A set that spans the space and is linearly independent

la_m2_dimension_meaning.title: Dimension
la_m2_dimension_meaning.prompt: What does the dimension of a vector space mean?
la_m2_dimension_meaning.hint: It is tied to basis size.

options.a: The number of vectors in any basis
options.c: The number of rows in any matrix
options.d: The norm of the zero vector

la_m2_standard_basis_r3.title: Standard basis in R^3
la_m2_standard_basis_r3.prompt: Which set is the standard basis for R^3?

la_m2_basis_count_plane.title: Basis size of a plane
la_m2_basis_count_plane.prompt: How many vectors are needed in a basis for a plane through the origin in R^3?
la_m2_basis_count_plane.hint: A plane has two independent directions.

la_m2_basis_independent_spanning.title: Independent and spanning
la_m2_basis_independent_spanning.prompt: If a set both spans a space and is linearly independent, then it is:

la_m2_transpose_column_to_row.title: Column becomes row
la_m2_transpose_column_to_row.prompt: What does transpose do to a column vector?

options.a: It turns it into a row vector

la_m2_determinant_2x2_numeric.title: 2×2 determinant
la_m2_determinant_2x2_numeric.hint: For a 2×2 matrix, use ad - bc.

la_m2_determinant_area_scaling.title: Determinant and area
la_m2_determinant_area_scaling.prompt: Geometrically, the determinant of a 2×2 matrix measures:

options.c: The length of the first column

la_m2_determinant_zero_noninvertible.title: Zero determinant
la_m2_determinant_zero_noninvertible.prompt: If det(A)=0 for a square matrix A, what follows?

la_m2_determinant_triangular_numeric.title: Triangular determinant
la_m2_determinant_triangular_numeric.hint: For triangular matrices, multiply the diagonal entries.

la_m2_determinant_swap_rows.title: Swapping rows
la_m2_determinant_swap_rows.prompt: What happens to the determinant when two rows are swapped?

la_m2_inverse_definition.title: Inverse definition
la_m2_inverse_definition.prompt: What makes a matrix B the inverse of A?
la_m2_inverse_definition.hint: Composition should give the identity both ways.

la_m2_inverse_identity.title: Inverse of the identity
la_m2_inverse_identity.prompt: What is the inverse of the identity matrix?

la_m2_inverse_exists_det.title: Existence of the inverse
la_m2_inverse_exists_det.prompt: A square matrix is invertible exactly when:

la_m2_inverse_2x2_input.title: Compute an inverse
la_m2_inverse_2x2_input.hint: You are entering A^{-1}, not A.

la_m2_inverse_solve_system.title: Inverse and solving systems
la_m2_inverse_solve_system.prompt: Why is an inverse useful in solving Ax=b?
la_m2_inverse_solve_system.hint: Multiply by A^{-1} if it exists.

options.c: Because x = bA^{-1} for any shape

la_m2_eigenvalue_definition.title: Eigenvalue definition
la_m2_eigenvalue_definition.prompt: In the equation Av = λv, the scalar λ is called:
la_m2_eigenvalue_definition.hint: It is the scale factor attached to the special direction.

la_m2_eigenvector_definition.title: Eigenvector definition
la_m2_eigenvector_definition.hint: It keeps its line under the matrix action.

options.c: A nonzero vector whose image is just a scalar multiple of itself
options.d: A vector orthogonal to every other vector

la_m2_eigen_diagonal_value_numeric.title: Eigenvalue of a diagonal matrix
la_m2_eigen_diagonal_value_numeric.hint: For diagonal matrices, the standard basis vectors pick out the diagonal entries.

la_m2_eigenvector_choice.title: Choose an eigenvector
la_m2_eigenvector_choice.hint: For a diagonal matrix, the coordinate axes are the special directions.

la_m2_eigen_triangular_values.title: Eigenvalues of a triangular matrix
la_m2_eigen_triangular_values.prompt: Where do the eigenvalues of a triangular matrix come from?

la_m2_eigenvector_not_zero.title: Why nonzero?
la_m2_eigenvector_not_zero.prompt: Why is the zero vector excluded from the definition of an eigenvector?
la_m2_eigenvector_not_zero.hint: Otherwise the definition would be trivial.

options.a: Because 0 would satisfy Av=λv for every λ and give no direction information
options.b: Because 0 is never in a vector space
